name: Tokio (Async Runtime for Rust)

performance.notes: Work-stealing multi-threaded scheduler; top-tier async performance

agent_readiness.notes: #[tokio::main] async fn main(). tokio::spawn for concurrent tasks. tokio::sync: Mutex, RwLock, mpsc, oneshot, broadcast channels. tokio::time: sleep, timeout, interval. tokio::fs, tokio::net (async TCP/UDP). Multi-threaded work-stealing scheduler. The de facto async runtime for Rust. Powers axum, sqlx, reqwest, hyper. Feature flags for minimal builds. Mini-tokio or async-std as alternatives. tokio-console for debugging. Tracing integration.

gotchas: Don't block the async executor (use tokio::task::spawn_blocking for CPU work)
Cannot use regular std::sync::Mutex in async code (deadlocks)
async fn in traits requires async_trait macro (pre-1.75 stable Rust)
Large binary size increase from async machinery
best_when: Any production async Rust application (axum, hyper, sqlx all require it)
High-concurrency network services needing thousands of connections
IO-bound Rust services (HTTP servers, proxies, clients)
avoid_when: Embedded systems without std (async-std or smol lighter)
Simple scripts (synchronous Rust may be simpler)
summary: Tokio is the de facto async runtime for Rust. #[tokio::main], tokio::spawn, sync primitives (Mutex, channels), time utilities, async fs/net. Work-stealing multi-threaded scheduler. Powers axum, sqlx, reqwest, hyper. Required by most serious Rust async ecosystems. Use Rayon for CPU parallelism; use tokio::task::spawn_blocking for CPU work within async context.
